name: Stoneware Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Stoneware. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Stoneware surface cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-infrared wavelength optimized for Stoneware mineral absorption

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning control

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage

energyDensity.description: Fluence level for effective contaminant removal without substrate damage

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation of contaminants

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning pattern

power.description: Typical range for industrial laser cleaning of ceramics like stoneware to avoid thermal damage; starting at 100W for light contamination removal. Max safe before substrate cracking or discoloration. Source: Manufacturer specs from CleanLaser systems and studies in Journal of Laser Applications (e.g., 2018 paper on ceramic surface preparation).

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds for 1064 nm cleaning on stoneware; slower for heavy contaminants (500-1000 mm/s) vs light (2000-3000 mm/s). Recommended 1500 mm/s balances efficiency and safety. Source: Industry standards from SPIE proceedings on laser cleaning (e.g., 2020) and equipment manuals from IPG Photonics.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for stoneware to ablate contaminants without deep penetration; picosecond (ps) for ultra-minimal HAZ but less common industrially. 20 ns recommended to limit heat accumulation in low-conductivity ceramics. Femtosecond avoided due to cost. Source: Laser physics research in Optics Express (e.g., 2015 study on pulsed laser ablation of ceramics).

repetition_rate.description: Typical for cleaning applications; 30 kHz recommended for stoneware to achieve fast coverage while minimizing heat buildup—higher rates (>50 kHz) risk accumulation on low-thermal-conductivity materials. Trade-off: speed vs thermal safety. Source: Academic literature in Applied Surface Science (e.g., 2019 on repetition rate effects in ceramic cleaning).

energy_density.description: Fluence range for contaminant removal on stoneware; damage threshold ~4 J/cm² due to high melting point (~1200°C) but conservative to prevent micro-cracks. 1.5 J/cm² safe for surface preparation. Stoneware's moderate reflectivity at 1064 nm requires mid-range. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Journal of Cultural Heritage (e.g., 2017 on stone and ceramic lasers).

spot_size.description: Beam diameter for uniform cleaning; smaller for fine details (200-400 μm), larger for broad areas (500-1000 μm). 500 μm recommended for stoneware industrial apps to cover surfaces efficiently without hotspots. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser systems for ceramic processing.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy; 2 passes recommended for complete stoneware cleaning to ensure removal without overexposure. Based on layered ablation approach. Source: Industry practice in case studies from Laser Institute of America reports (e.g., 2021 industrial cleaning guidelines).

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage; 50% recommended for stoneware to avoid gaps or excessive overlap-induced damage, adjustable 30% for speed or 80% for thoroughness. Typical range balances efficiency. Source: Process optimization studies in Optics and Lasers in Engineering (e.g., 2016 on scan overlap in surface cleaning).
